# Agentic Flow v2.3.0 - run log

**Date:** 2026-04-03
**Branch:** `v2.3.0`
**Scope:** Logging template standardization and run observability improvements
**Compared to:** `v2.2.0`

---

## Version Updates

### Change Summary

Use this section to describe what changed in this version compared to the previous one.
Prefer concrete, implementation-focused entries.

| Area | How it was | What is changed | What is now |
|------|------------|-----------------|-------------|
| Log template / observability | Run logs evolved organically across versions and had no single standard structure for comparing changes, findings, costs, and outcomes | Added a reusable `.github/agentic-flow/log-template.md` and used it as the baseline for new logs | Run logs now have a unified structure with explicit version updates, user observations, agent analysis, and cost/context tracking to improve observability |
| Code styling / consistency | Code formatting depended on manual habits, and several quality expectations from `documentation/code-guidance.md` still relied mainly on reviewer judgment instead of repo-enforced tooling | Added Spotless with `palantir-java-format` for `flow-orchestrator`, introduced a user-friendly `scripts/final-check.sh` wrapper for the final flow, aligned `mcp-server` formatting checks with Prettier, tightened Checkstyle naming/finality coverage, re-enabled SpotBugs defensive-copy enforcement, and removed the now-tool-enforced local-variable-finality rule from `code-guidance.md` | Agents and users now have a single final-check command for formatting plus verification, stronger automatic enforcement of code-quality and consistency rules, less reviewer-only interpretation for objective checks, and a cleaner guidance document focused on rules that still need human judgment |
| Project structure / naming conventions | Project structure and naming patterns had already improved in code, but the documentation still described them only partially and did not clearly define the naming suffixes used across layers | Updated `documentation/code-guidance.md` to document root package responsibilities, capability-root class placement, and naming conventions for `Service`, `Port`, `Adapter`, `Mapper`, `Properties`, `Request`, `Response`, `Dto`, and `Input`; aligned the high-level structure note in `.github/copilot-instructions.md` | The documented architecture now matches the current codebase more closely, which should reduce ambiguity in agent decisions and make package placement and naming more predictable |
| Agentic-flow review agent | Flow-level review relied mainly on the generic orchestration prompt and existing reviewer agents, which did not give a dedicated reviewer a fixed starting context for comparing the current run with the previous iteration | Added `.github/agents/flow-reviewer.agent.md` as a dedicated GitHub Copilot custom agent based on `.github/prompts/agentic-orchestration.prompt.md`; defined required context files, log selection rules, evidence-checking scope, and review output structure; set the model to `Claude Opus 4.6` | The flow can now be reviewed with a purpose-built Opus reviewer that starts from the right files, checks the latest and previous run logs explicitly, and produces more consistent, evidence-based agentic-flow analysis |
| Coder responsibility split (P1-A) | Coder agent had 12+ responsibilities per invocation (implement, test, verify, start app, curl, update smoke-test.sh, write verification log, update implementation report, parse Maven output). This caused terminal mega-commands, stalls, and missed handoffs across 4 consecutive versions | Stripped Coder to 5 core duties: implement, test, `verify-quick.sh` per slice, `final-check.sh` before handoff, return status. Moved app startup, smoke tests, verification log writing, and implementation report evidence to Team Lead. Moved smoke-test.sh design to Architect. Updated `java-coder.agent.md`, `team-lead.agent.md`, `java-architect.agent.md`, `local-quality-rules.instructions.md`, and `agentic-flow-overview.md` | Coder now has a focused prompt that should reduce context rot, eliminate mega-commands, and produce faster handoffs. TL consolidates all evidence-gathering. Architect owns smoke-test design since it has the API and payload context |
| Reviewer code quality + triple verification (P1-B, P2-A) | Reviewer Phase 2 was an expensive terminal runner: re-ran `verify-quick.sh`, `final-check.sh`, startup, and smoke tests without performing meaningful code review. In v2.2.0 it missed every code quality issue the user caught manually. Three agents ran overlapping verification commands, wasting ~6-8% of context per feature | Rewrote Reviewer Phase 2: replaced mandatory script re-execution with evidence validation (re-run only if TL evidence is suspect); added explicit Code Quality Review checklist (naming consistency, model count, duplicate patterns, over-engineering, log context, test quality) referencing `code-guidance.md`; removed startup and smoke-test re-execution entirely. Updated `reviewer.agent.md`, `local-quality-rules.instructions.md`, and `agentic-flow-overview.md` | Reviewer now focuses on code quality judgment that tools cannot provide, validates TL evidence instead of duplicating it, and uses less context per run. Each verification command is owned by exactly one agent |
| Agent model updates for copilot-cli | Models were set for VS Code IDE names only, preventing copilot-cli usage. PM was on Sonnet 4.5, Architect was on Sonnet 4.6 | Updated all 5 agent files to copilot-cli model names (`chatgpt`, `claude-sonnet-4.6`, `claude-opus-4.6`, `codex-5.3`, `gemini-3.1-pro`); kept IDE names as inline comments for easy switching. Upgraded PM from Sonnet 4.5 → 4.6 and Architect from Sonnet 4.6 → Opus 4.6 | Agents can now run in copilot-cli. Architect gets Opus-level reasoning for more complex plans. IDE names preserved for quick switch-back |
| Context map for targeted exploration | Architect and Coder scanned the full codebase to discover structure, wasting context on files unrelated to their task. As capabilities grow this becomes a major context-rot driver | Created `documentation/context-map.md` listing capabilities, packages, classes, endpoints, config, tests, and design notes. Wired into Architect and Coder preparation steps (read context-map first, then only target files). Added TL duty to update the map after every feature delivery. Added context-map as first Key Reference in `copilot-instructions.md` | Agents locate target code in ~50 lines instead of scanning 100+ files. Scales as capabilities are added. Human-readable too |
| Code refactoring (pre third-run) | `GitLabIssuesAdapter` contained repeated project-path resolution, optional query-param null checks, duplicated try/catch blocks, unnecessary `requireNonNull` wrappers, and class-level `@SuppressWarnings("null")` in tests | Extracted `GitLabUriFactory` for shared URI building, added `addOptionalParam(...)` helper, centralized `executeGitLabOperation(...)` for exception handling, removed unnecessary wrappers and test suppressions, improved create/delete logging context. Full report: `artifacts/refactoring/v2.3.0-refactor.report.md` | Adapter code is cleaner, DRY, and easier to extend for new capabilities. Tests are 77 green, all quality gates pass. This was a standalone refactoring unrelated to the second run |
| Smoke testing tooling (pre third-run) | Smoke tests relied on a brittle `scripts/smoke-test.sh` bash script using `curl` with manual status-code parsing, field-presence grep checks, and inline JSON payloads. Agents struggled with curl quoting, error handling, and maintaining the script as APIs evolved | Introduced Karate 2.0 as the structured API smoke-test framework. Added `src/test/karate/` test root with `.feature` files for health, search, create, delete, and lifecycle scenarios. Created `IssuesKarateTest` runner, `karate-config.js` with env-aware `baseUrl`, isolated Karate via Maven `-Pkarate` profile (excluded from surefire + default failsafe), and added `scripts/karate-test.sh` wrapper. Deprecated `scripts/smoke-test.sh`. Updated all agent files, `agentic-flow-overview.md`, `local-quality-rules.instructions.md`, `test-instructions.instructions.md`, and `context-map.md` to promote Karate as the default smoke-test tool and define agent responsibilities: Architect writes `.feature` files, TL executes against running app, Coder does NOT write or run Karate tests | Smoke tests are now structured, readable, assertion-rich Karate scenarios instead of fragile bash/curl scripts. Karate is fully isolated from unit and quality-gate runs. The Architect-writes-tests / TL-executes split keeps Coder focused on production code and unit/integration/component tests only |
| Agent prompt refinement (pre third-run) | Agent prompts had accumulated redundant instructions, inconsistent naming, and stale references across versions. TL prompt was 161 lines with duplicated context sections. Coder had overlapping must-not rules. Architect was blocked from writing Karate tests despite owning smoke-test design. Reviewer lacked handoff metadata and had vague rerun criteria. PM filename was inconsistent with other agents. Code-review-refactorer still referenced deprecated `smoke-test.sh` | Compressed TL prompt from 161 to 132 lines by merging context sections and tightening coder recheck and Phase 2 brief. Merged duplicate Coder must-not rules. Updated Architect to explicitly allow Karate `.feature` file writing. Added Reviewer handoffs frontmatter and defined concrete "suspect evidence" heuristic. Renamed `product-manager.md` to `product-manager.agent.md` and updated links. Fixed code-review-refactorer `smoke-test.sh` references to `karate-test.sh`. Updated Coder model to Sonnet 4.6 for third run | Leaner agent prompts with less redundancy, clearer Karate ownership, consistent file naming, and more precise Reviewer rerun criteria — all aimed at reducing context waste and improving agent focus in the next run |
| Delivery flow instructions consolidation (pre third-run) | Delivery-flow-specific rules were scattered across 3 sources: `blocker-escalation.instructions.md` (blocker rules, `applyTo: **`), `evidence-rules.instructions.md` (verification evidence, `applyTo: **/flow-orchestrator/**`), and the "Shared Workflow Rules" section in `copilot-instructions.md` (workflow control). None had `description` frontmatter to help the model decide relevance | Merged all three into a single `delivery-flow-rules.instructions.md` with `applyTo: **`, a clear `description` targeting delivery flow agents, and three subsections: Workflow Control, Blocker Escalation, Evidence And Verification. Deleted the two old files. Removed the Shared Workflow Rules section from `copilot-instructions.md`. Added `description` fields to all remaining instruction files (`secret-redaction`, `local-quality-rules`, `test-instructions`) | One canonical instruction file for all delivery flow cross-agent rules. Cleaner `copilot-instructions.md` focused on project-level context. All instruction files now have descriptions for better model relevance hints |
| Local quality rules refactoring (pre third-run) | `local-quality-rules.instructions.md` was 100 lines with duplicated command descriptions (scripts described 3 times across Preferred Commands, Toolchain, and Review Guidance sections), verbose formatting and PMD/SpotBugs guidance sections, and agent-specific workflow rules that overlapped with agent prompts | Refactored to ~45 lines: merged all command descriptions into a single table, consolidated evidence recording rules, kept report outputs and quality config paths. Removed duplicate Review Guidance, Formatting Expectations, PMD Ruleset Guidance, and Coverage Gate sections. Added verification reference in `copilot-instructions.md` linking to the instruction file. Simplified Coder `Must` section to reference the instruction file instead of repeating script names. Simplified code-review-refactorer verification section to reference the instruction | Halved the instruction file size. Universal verification baseline in `copilot-instructions.md` ensures all agents follow the scripts. Agent prompts reference the instruction instead of duplicating script commands |
| Architect slice upper bound (post third-run) | Architect slice sizing principle from F-2 only had a lower bound ("resist many slices") with no upper bound. Run 3 Architect produced 1 monolithic slice with ~20 files, causing 4+ coder retries and ~1 hour of wasted time | Added upper-bound rule to Architect slice rules: a single slice touching more than ~8-10 files (production + test combined) should be split even if the work is mechanical | Architect now has both floor and ceiling for slice sizing, preventing overcorrection in either direction |
| Reviewer model change (post third-run) | Reviewer was on Codex 5.3 which consistently struggled with structured evidence validation and defaulted to terminal exploration, git commands, and redundant check re-runs across runs 2 and 3 | Changed Reviewer model from `GPT-5.3-Codex (copilot)` to `Claude Sonnet 4.6 (copilot)` in `reviewer.agent.md` | Reviewer model is now consistent with Coder (Sonnet 4.6), which has shown better structured artifact writing and less terminal-obsessive behavior |
| Reviewer Phase 1 constitution crosscheck (post third-run) | Reviewer Phase 1 did not explicitly cross-reference story constraints against `constitution.md`. In run 3, PM wrote a mapping constraint that contradicted constitution Principles 2 and 8, and Phase 1 passed it through to the plan unchallenged | Added three new Phase 1 checks: (1) cross-reference every story constraint against constitution and blocker on conflict, (2) verify plan prose↔class-table consistency, (3) validate structural correctness of Architect-written Karate `.feature` files (syntax and assertion forms, no execution) | Phase 1 now catches story↔constitution conflicts, internal plan contradictions, and invalid test artifacts before coding starts — preventing the exact failure chain that caused the run 3 architectural issue and the Karate red card |
| Global git ban for all flow agents (post third-run) | Only TL had an explicit "Never run git commands" rule. In run 3, Reviewer (Codex) ran git commands despite this being prohibited in the delivery flow spirit | Added `[CRITICAL]` git ban covering all flow agents to `delivery-flow-rules.instructions.md` § Workflow Control. TL retains its local reinforcement | Version control is now explicitly blocked for every agent in the flow, not just TL |
| PM constitution awareness (post third-run) | PM had no instruction to read `constitution.md` before writing locked constraints. In run 3, PM wrote a mapping-boundary constraint ("mapping must happen in orchestration") that directly contradicted constitution Principles 2 and 8 | Added PM instruction to read `constitution.md` before writing constraints and blocker on conflict | PM can no longer write locked constraints that contradict the architecture without being instructed to escalate |
| Constitution composition rule (post third-run) | `constitution.md` had no explicit rule governing whether integration adapters may internally call multiple external endpoints. Run 3 confusion originated from unclear boundary between "who composes multi-source data" | Added [MUST] rule under Principle 2: orchestration service coordinates multi-source calls; an integration adapter maps one external call to one orchestration contract | Clear ownership boundary for composition, prevents story constraints from directing multi-call composition into integration adapters |
| IssueDetail → EnrichedIssueDetail refactoring (post third-run) | `IssueDetail` contained a `changeSets` field (always `List.of()`) set in the integration mapper, violating the new composition rule. The integration layer was forced to compose a placeholder for data that will come from a different external source in Story 2 | Removed `changeSets` from `IssueDetail` (10 fields). Created `EnrichedIssueDetail(IssueDetail, List<Object> changeSets)` in orchestration layer. Updated `IssuesService`, `IssuesResponseMapper`, `IssuesController`, `GitLabIssueDetailMapper`, and all corresponding tests | Integration adapter returns pure single-source data; composition happens in orchestration. Story 2 (label events) will populate `changeSets` via a second port call composed in `IssuesService` |
| Architect scanning discipline (post third-run) | Architect spent ~20 minutes reading dozens of unrelated files (other features' plans, reports, reviews, unrelated source files) before writing the plan. This wasted ~33% of the Architect's runtime and contributed to context rot | Tightened Architect "Must not" rules: no reading other features' implementation artifacts; no exploring source files outside packages identified in `context-map.md`. Rewrote execution protocol with 8 prescriptive steps: checkpoint → story → context-map → constitution/code-guidance → targeted source files → verify external APIs → choose structure → write plan | Architect has a strict navigation funnel: checkpoint → context-map → relevant files only. Should eliminate wasteful exploration and reduce planning time from ~20 min to ~5-8 min |
| Context-map update ownership (post third-run) | `context-map.md` was maintained by TL at sign-off. Reviewer Phase 2 caught stale entries and red-carded the Coder, causing an extra round-trip. TL verification happened too late in the pipeline to prevent wasted time | Made Coder responsible for updating `context-map.md` during implementation (added to pre-handoff checklist step 5). Added context-map freshness to Reviewer Phase 2 code quality checklist. TL sign-off gate unchanged (still verifies) | Context-map is now updated by the agent that creates/changes files, validated by the Reviewer, and verified by TL — catching staleness early instead of at sign-off |
| Context-map zero-scan rewrite (post third-run) | `context-map.md` listed class names by package but not full file paths. Agents still needed `list_dir` / `file_search` to locate files, which cascaded into scanning unrelated directories. Missing entries: `GitLabUriFactory`, component test support classes, Karate feature files, individual test files, `FlowOrchestratorApplication`, config YAML files. Maintenance rule still said "Team Lead" after ownership was moved to Coder | Rewrote context-map as a file-level lookup table: every production class, test file, Karate feature, config file, and script has its own row with relative path and role. Added sections for application entry point, configuration files, and test infrastructure. Fixed maintenance rule to show three-tier ownership (Coder updates → Reviewer validates → TL verifies). Added `GitLabUriFactory` to shared infra and key patterns | Agents can now go directly from context-map to `read_file` without any directory exploration. No `list_dir` or `file_search` needed for known capabilities. Should eliminate the root cause of Architect scanning |

### Addressed Findings From Previous Log Analysis                                       

List how the issues and suggestions from the previous log were handled in this version.
If one issue required multiple actions, list them in the same cell as separate bullets.

| Issue spotted | How was addressed | What pain point should be solved |
|---------------|-------------------|----------------------------------|
| Gap 1 — Coder is the bottleneck (12+ responsibilities, mega-commands, stalls) | - Removed app startup, curl/smoke checks, smoke-test.sh updates, verification log writing, implementation report evidence from Coder<br>- Simplified evidence gathering to `PASS`/`FAIL` reporting<br>- Simplified handoff to 5-field summary<br>- Moved evidence-gathering duties to Team Lead<br>- Moved smoke-test design to Architect | Fewer coder loops, no mega-commands, less context pressure, faster handoffs |
| Gap 4 — Excessive TL↔Coder ping-pong from triple verification | - Replaced TL `mvn test` with `scripts/final-check.sh` as single recheck<br>- TL now runs final-check once per batch (not overlapping with Coder)<br>- Reviewer no longer re-runs startup or smoke tests; validates TL evidence instead | Clearer ownership: each verification command owned by exactly one agent |
| Gap 5 — Coder terminal mega-commands | - Replaced structured log-writing instruction with simple `PASS`/`FAIL` reporting<br>- Removed verification log section header requirement from Coder | No incentive to build bash parsers; evidence writing is TL's job |
| Gap 3 — Reviewer adds no code quality value | - Added Code Quality Review checklist to Reviewer Phase 2: naming consistency, model count, duplicate patterns, over-engineering, log context, test quality<br>- Replaced mandatory script re-execution with evidence validation<br>- Reviewer re-runs `final-check.sh` only when TL evidence is suspect | Reviewer focuses on judgment-based code review instead of being an expensive terminal runner |
| Gap 10 — No formatting tool | Added Spotless with `palantir-java-format`, `scripts/format-code.sh`, `scripts/final-check.sh` | Formatting issues eliminated permanently |
| Gap 6 — No package structure reference | Added Package Structure And Model Gate to `code-guidance.md` | Architect and Coder follow documented structure |
| Gap 8 — Command naming pattern | Added `*Input` suffix convention, renamed `CreateIssueCommand` → `CreateIssueInput` | No misleading CQRS implications |
| Run observability gap | Created `log-template.md` as reusable run log baseline | Structured, comparable analysis across versions |
| Reviewer flow-level analysis gap | Added `flow-reviewer.agent.md` dedicated agent | Purpose-built reviewer for system-level analysis |

## Run Notes (manually populated by user)

### First small feature run in CLI

- Started with copilot-cli at 15:40 - it actually affected the whole flow, needed to be re-checked with the second run in IDE as all models were picked up wrongly!! Architect was Sonnet (we already tried this model for the run) (but let's see, maybe smth obvious to fix will come out of this run)
- Issues - wrong model was picked up for team lead right ahead (sonnet)
- Looks like all models were decided by team lead, not an agent files - wrong names???
- Story was confrmed from 1 attempt (hoiku worked)
- Architect's plan confirmed from the first attempt (Sonnet worked, but still not opus)
- Architect plan had 6!!! slices for 1 field in 2 APIs update - that looks too extensive for such functionality, we need clear guidance on slice size otherwise it's loosing requests without obvious reasons
- Review phase 1 passed
- Context at this moment 27%, 10mins run
- First batch is returned to TL after Coder has implemented it (Coder is also Sonnet)
- TL ran checks and confirmed, then moced to coder with batch 2.
- 31% context used, 20 mins run
- Coder finished batch 2 cleanly, no red card, TL accepted the batch and moved to the last one
- Checked the plan - it looks too explicit for such small change - looks like model produces the same amount of lines no matter if it needs to implement the whole app or update logging... We need to check how to make it more reasonable - otherwise it's the whole for waisinting resources - we needed 1 batch for such small story, but got 3 batches.
- Coder passed last batch to TL, TL does the checks - all verifications passed
- Context 34%, 25 mins run
- TL passed to Reviewer for Phase 2 checks
- Reviewer returned back to TL as passed
- TL runs spot checks - do we now actually need than as TL just before reviewer ran final ones?
- TL confirms that it's ok and starting to work on sign-off
- TL created sign-off and started to commit the changes!!! Why? We don't need it - re-check Team Lead instructions - that's dangerous
- Signoff completed - context used 45%, time - 38mins. Both metrics are too high for such small feature. The main question is - workflow in such set up is not suitable for small stories or it's an issue of current setup. We should retest on normally sized feature and check what happens with the metrics.

## Mid-Work Analysis: Issues From First Run And Second Run Plan

**Analyzed by:** GitHub Copilot (Claude Opus 4.6)
**Analysis date:** 2026-04-06
**Purpose:** Trace user-highlighted issues to root causes, plan concrete fixes, and define a second-run approach for a normally sized feature inside IDE.

### Issues Identified In First Run

| # | Issue | Evidence from run notes | Root cause | Severity |
|---|-------|------------------------|------------|----------|
| I-1 | Wrong model pickup — all agents ran Sonnet instead of configured models | "wrong model was picked up for team lead right ahead (sonnet)", "all models were decided by team lead, not agent files" | `copilot-cli` model name resolution differs from IDE; the TL `chatgpt` model name likely falls back to a default or the CLI resolves all sub-agent calls through the TL's own model tier | HIGH — invalidates the entire run as a model comparison test |
| I-2 | Excessive plan — 6 slices for adding 1 field to 2 existing APIs | "Architect plan had 6!!! slices for 1 field in 2 APIs update", "plan - it looks too explicit for such small change" | Architect prompt says "small, ordered, executable slices" without any proportionality guidance. The model fills the template structure regardless of feature size. No slice-count heuristic exists | HIGH — directly caused 3 batches, 3x TL↔Coder handoffs, and 45% context usage |
| I-3 | Too many batches — 3 coder batches for a trivial feature | "we needed 1 batch for such small story, but got 3 batches" | TL is hard-coded to max 2 slices per coder invocation. With 6 slices, that's 3 mandatory batches. No dynamic batch sizing exists | MEDIUM — consequence of I-2, but batch rule also needs flexibility |
| I-4 | TL spot checks after Reviewer are redundant | "TL runs spot checks - do we now actually need than as TL just before reviewer ran final ones?" | TL instructions mandate spot checks after Reviewer Phase 2, but TL already ran `final-check.sh` + startup + smoke tests before Reviewer. The post-Reviewer spot checks duplicate terminal work | LOW — small cost, but signals unclear ownership |
| I-5 | TL attempted to commit changes | "TL created sign-off and started to commit the changes!!! Why? We don't need it" | TL agent has no explicit prohibition on git operations. The line "Do not rely on git commits in this flow" is a soft hint about batch boundaries, not a ban on committing. The model inferred commit as part of sign-off completion | **CRITICAL** — destructive action, must be blocked |
| I-6 | 45% context / 38 min for tiny feature | Metrics in Post Run Checks | Compound effect of I-1 (wrong models), I-2 (6 slices), I-3 (3 batches), plus verification overhead at every batch boundary | HIGH — makes the workflow unsuitable for small stories at current settings |

### Planned Fixes For Second Run

#### Fix F-1: Prevent TL from running git commands (addresses I-5) — APPLIED

**Priority:** CRITICAL
**File:** `.github/agents/team-lead.agent.md`
**Change:** Added to `## Boundaries` section:
```
- **[CRITICAL]** Never run git commands (commit, push, add, branch, checkout, reset). The user manages version control outside this workflow.
```

#### Fix F-2: Add slice sizing principle to Architect (addresses I-2) — APPLIED

**Priority:** HIGH
**File:** `.github/agents/java-architect.agent.md`
**Change:** Added a principle-based constraint after the 200-line cap. Instead of a rule-based file-count threshold, the instruction teaches the model *why* slices exist and when fewer slices are correct:
```
- **Slice sizing principle:** Slices exist to isolate implementation risk and provide meaningful
  verification checkpoints — not to decompose a plan into the maximum number of steps. Before
  defining slices, assess the overall scope: how many layers are affected, how much new logic is
  introduced, and how much of the change is mechanical (adding a field, wiring an existing pattern)
  versus genuinely new behavior. Mechanical changes that follow an established codebase pattern
  should be grouped together, not split into separate slices. A slice must represent a unit of work
  where something new can go wrong and needs independent verification. If the entire feature is a
  straightforward extension of existing patterns, one slice is the correct answer. Resist the urge
  to produce a longer plan when a shorter one is sufficient.
```
**Expected impact:** Architect produces plans proportional to actual complexity, not template length. Works for any feature type.

#### Fix F-3: Dynamic batch sizing for TL (addresses I-3) — DROPPED

**Reason:** Dropped per user decision. Large slices can confuse Coder when batched together. The 2-slice-per-batch cap stays as a safety net. The real fix is F-2 — fewer slices means fewer batches naturally.

#### Fix F-4: Clarify post-Reviewer spot checks are read-only (addresses I-4) — APPLIED

**Priority:** LOW
**File:** `.github/agents/team-lead.agent.md`
**Change:** Added to "Reviewer Audit And Red Card" section:
```
- Spot checks are read-only: review code, artifacts, and Reviewer reasoning. Do NOT re-run terminal
  verification commands (final-check.sh, verify-quick.sh, startup, smoke tests) that were already
  executed before Reviewer Phase 2.
```
**Expected impact:** Eliminates 1 redundant `final-check.sh` execution per run.

#### Fix F-5: IDE run with explicit model assignment (addresses I-1) — RUN CONFIG

**Priority:** HIGH — run configuration, no file changes
**Action for second run:**
- Run inside VS Code IDE, not copilot-cli
- Assign TL model to `Claude Opus 4.6 (copilot)` to test the theory that `chatgpt` can't properly invoke Opus sub-agents
- Keep other agent models as currently configured (Architect: Opus, Coder: Codex, PM: Haiku, Reviewer: Gemini)
- Monitor which models actually execute at each stage

### Second Run Configuration

| Parameter | Value | Rationale |
|-----------|-------|-----------|
| Environment | VS Code IDE | CLI had model resolution issues |
| Feature size | Normal (new API endpoint or multi-layer change) | Small feature was insufficient to stress-test the workflow |
| TL model | `Claude Opus 4.6 (copilot)` | Test Opus-as-orchestrator theory; verify sub-agent invocation |
| Architect model | `Claude Opus 4.6 (copilot)` | Keep as-is; worked in first run |
| Coder model | `GPT-5.3-Codex (copilot)` | Keep as-is; give it another chance with simplified plan |
| PM model | Haiku | Keep as-is; worked first try |
| Reviewer model | `Gemini 3.1 Pro (Preview) (copilot)` | Keep as-is |
| Fixes to apply before run | F-1 (CRITICAL), F-2 (HIGH), F-3 (MEDIUM), F-4 (LOW) | All are prompt changes, low risk |

### Second Run: Observation Checklist

#### User Notes:
- Started at 16:48 with opus as Lead.
- Opus returned back with clarification questions, which is good sign.
- Only after clarification Opus moved to requirement lock
- TL passed to PM for story creation (model is picked up correctly now - Sonnet)
- First issue spotted - Opus uses significantly more context - 32% till this point and we are at the beginning only.
- PM returned a story which was accepted, TL moved to Architect. And in this set up Architect was selected correctly - as Opus as well. Looks like clear bug (or undocumented behaviour) from github - Opus clearly can be set as a model only if calling model is 3x.
- Plan is created and accepted by TL. The result is passed to Reviewer for Phae 1 checks. Reviewer model is chosen correctly.
- At this point 36% of context is used. 14 mins run.

From code perspective - my main concern is incorrect modeling - orchestrator change model doesn't have all fields, and they added only on DTO which is extremely bad pattern and looks dirty and unmaintainable. We hould rather have clear Change interface which requires field (enum, with single label value for now), changedBy and other fields. Dto should take is 1-1, this logic should never change just on Dto level. So the flow is: client API requests issue dtails -> orchestrator asks integration to fetch data (separately for issue details and label events), it's orchestration responsibility. Integration fetches the data, map it to Orchestration model and returns to Orchestrator. Orchestrator combines the proper response to full orchestration model (not Dto), then controller maps it to DTO. In most cases before actually pass it to controller we will have domain logic to run to evaluate the issues. Also I\m concerned how the fetch happens - we should do it in parallel, not sequentially.

## Agent Analysis

**Analyzed by:** GitHub Copilot (Claude Opus 4.6)
**Analysis date:** 2026-04-08
**Scope:** Full run analysis across all 4 runs in v2.3.0, linked artifacts, generated code, v2.3.3 code review, and comparison with v2.2.0

### 1. Executive Summary

v2.3.0 was a **substantial improvement** in workflow design and tooling maturity, but the core delivery loop — Coder execution + Reviewer Phase 2 — remains the dominant bottleneck and the primary drag on time, context, and code quality. Across 4 runs, the planning phase was consistently fast and reliable (8-14 min, 7-11% context). The implementation phase was consistently expensive and unpredictable (30-80 min, 15-30% context), with the same two failure modes repeating in runs 3 and 4: (1) Coder handoff incompleteness triggering TL rejection loops, and (2) Reviewer Phase 2 re-running all checks on documentation-only fixes instead of scoping to the delta.

The workflow produced **functionally correct code** in all 4 runs — all quality gates green, API verified, tests passing. However, the **architectural quality** of generated code degraded across runs 3 and 4: the same constitution violation (mapping in integration layer instead of orchestration) recurred in run 4 despite being explicitly fixed after run 3 and despite explicit story constraints. This required a full post-run code review and refactoring cycle (v2.3.3), which produced 2 CRITICAL, 1 HIGH, and 2 MEDIUM findings.

**Top 5 takeaways:**

1. **Planning phase is solved.** PM (Haiku) + Architect (Sonnet 4.6) + Phase 1 Reviewer is now a reliable, fast, context-efficient pipeline.
2. **Coder handoff completeness is the #1 time sink.** In runs 2, 3, and 4, Coder failed to produce complete handoff artifacts on first attempt — not code failures, but missing report files, verification logs, and context-map updates.
3. **Reviewer Phase 2 lacks delta awareness.** When sent back for a documentation-only fix (runs 3 and 4), the Reviewer restarts the entire checklist from scratch. This wasted 15-20 min in run 3 and 10 min in run 4.
4. **Architecture violations persist despite explicit constraints.** The `Change`/`ChangeSet` model hierarchy, DTO contract, and sequential fetching issues in run 4 show that code-guidance rules alone do not override the models' learned patterns. Stronger structural enforcement or pre-implementation scaffolding is needed.
5. **Run time is the critical metric.** Context usage improved significantly (38-45% for complete features), but wall-clock time (1h20 for run 4, 2h for run 3) remains well above the 30-40 min target for medium-sized features.

### 2. Run Snapshot

| Dimension | Result | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Outcome quality | **GOOD** | Functionally correct in all 4 runs; all gates green; API verified |
| Requirement fidelity | **PARTIAL** | Story constraints delivered functionally but architecture rules violated in runs 3+4 (mapping in wrong layer, incomplete model contracts) |
| Code quality | **NEEDS REFACTORING** | v2.3.3 code review found 2 CRITICAL + 1 HIGH + 2 MEDIUM issues; all refactored post-run |
| Verification reliability | **IMPROVED** | Quality gates catch real failures; Karate works; no false-positive passes. But Reviewer adds limited code-quality value |
| Agent coordination | **MIXED** | Planning clean; implementation phase has 1-4 TL↔Coder round-trips per run |
| Tooling effectiveness | **STRONG** | `final-check.sh`, `karate-test.sh`, `verify-quick.sh`, Spotless all working reliably |
| Context efficiency | **IMPROVED** | Run 1: 45% (small feature), Run 2: 53→compacted (with Opus TL), Run 3: 35%, Run 4: 38% |
| Time efficiency | **NEEDS WORK** | Run 1: 38min (tiny feature), Run 2: 40min, Run 3: 2h, Run 4: 1h20m. Target: 30-40min |

### 3. Observation Analysis

#### 3.1 What Worked Well

1. **PM (Haiku) is fastest and most reliable agent.** Story created in <1 min in runs 3 and 4. Never rejected after run 2. Cheapest model, consistent output. The constitution-awareness fix (F-11) worked: no more contradictory constraints after run 3.

2. **Architect (Sonnet 4.6) improved significantly.** File scanning reduced 20-30% from run 3 to run 4 (user observation). Plan created in 6 min in run 4 vs 20 min in run 3. The context-map zero-scan rewrite and "must not read other features' artifacts" rules had measurable impact. Slice sizing improved: 3 slices in run 4 vs 1 monolithic slice in run 3 (which caused 4+ coder retries).

3. **Phase 1 Reviewer is now a genuine quality gate.** Constitution crosscheck (F-8), plan prose↔class-table consistency check, and Karate syntax validation were all added post-run-3. Phase 1 passed on first attempt in run 4 with thorough evidence in `label-events-api.review.json`.

4. **Karate test infrastructure works.** `karate-test.sh` with auto-start/reuse is a significant improvement over the v2.2.0 `smoke-test.sh` curl approach. Structured assertions, readable scenarios, proper isolation from unit tests. Saved 3-5 min per smoke test execution (user: "Significant improvement was to create a script which checks if app is running").

5. **TL quality gates function correctly.** TL correctly rejected incomplete coder handoffs (runs 2-4), ran independent `final-check.sh`, and did not advance to Phase 2 with unverified evidence. No false-positive passes. The red card system worked: run 3 Karate failure was correctly sent back to Coder with evidence.

6. **Coder responsibility simplification had partial effect.** No more mega-commands in runs 3-4 (compared to v2.2.0). Coder no longer attempts to parse Maven output with bash. However, the handoff artifact completeness problem persists.

7. **Formatting is a solved problem.** Spotless + `format-code.sh` + `final-check.sh` eliminated all manual formatting concerns permanently.

#### 3.2 What Failed or Regressed

##### 3.2.1 CRITICAL — Architecture violations repeat despite explicit constraints (Runs 3 + 4)

**What happened:** In run 3, the `GitLabIssueDetailMapper` was placed in the integration layer despite the story explicitly stating mapping logic belongs in orchestration. In run 4, the same pattern recurred: `Change` interface was underspecified (single `field()` method), `ChangeSetDto` was an empty marker interface, and the DTO layer diverged from the domain model. The v2.3.3 code review found 2 CRITICAL findings:
- `Change` interface lacked `id()` and `name()` — unusable as a polymorphic contract
- `LabelChangeDto` had fields absent from the `Change` contract, making DTO the de-facto model

**Why it happened:** Models have strong learned patterns about where to place mappers and how to structure interfaces. `code-guidance.md` defines rules, but the Architect and Coder both default to "whatever the model has seen most." The constitution says "each adapter method maps one external call to one orchestration contract" but doesn't prescribe interface contract completeness. The Reviewer Phase 2 did not catch these issues despite having a code quality checklist.

**Impact:** Required a full post-run refactoring cycle (v2.3.3-refactor.report.md): enriched `Change` contract with `ChangeField` enum, aligned DTO to domain model with sealed interfaces, parallelized fetching with `CompletableFuture`, added missing edge-case tests. This is the highest-cost failure mode: the workflow produces "green" code that passes all automated checks but has structural flaws that require human review.

##### 3.2.2 HIGH — Coder handoff artifact incompleteness (Runs 2, 3, 4)

**What happened:** In all three implementation runs, Coder failed to produce complete handoff artifacts on the first attempt:
- Run 2: "coder didn't return the required handoff summary" — TL rejected
- Run 3: 4 coder rejections — implementation report missing, verification log missing, handoff not returned
- Run 4: Reviewer Phase 2 rejected for "incomplete context-map coverage and implementation-report deviation contradiction"

**Why it happened:** Handoff completeness requires the Coder to: (1) implement code, (2) write tests, (3) run verification, (4) update implementation report, (5) update context-map, (6) return structured handoff. Steps 4-6 are administrative and peripheral to the coding task. The model deprioritizes them, especially as context pressure increases. This is not model-specific — both Codex (runs 2-3) and Sonnet (run 3 attempt) exhibited the same behavior.

**Impact:** Each rejected handoff costs ~5-10 min (TL verification + rejection + Coder re-read + fix + re-verify). In run 3, this accounted for ~40 min of a 2h run. In run 4, it added ~15 min.

##### 3.2.3 HIGH — Reviewer Phase 2 lacks delta awareness (Runs 3 + 4)

**What happened:**
- Run 3: Reviewer found context-map mismatch, TL sent to Coder for fix, Coder fixed, TL sent back to Reviewer. Reviewer "started all checks again" (user observation). Then failed again on a documentation typo. Another full re-run. Total Reviewer Phase 2: ~25 min for what should have been a 5 min code review.
- Run 4: Reviewer Phase 2 rejected for context-map and report issues. After Coder fix, Reviewer "didn't start from scratch, just revalidated findings in docs" — this was an improvement, but only because Sonnet 4.6 (run 4) behaved more sensibly than Codex 5.3 (run 3).

**Why it happened:** The Reviewer prompt has no mechanism for scoped re-review. When re-invoked, it re-reads the checkpoint and starts from its Phase 2 checklist. Whether it scopes down depends on the model's interpretation, not the instructions. Codex systematically re-ran everything; Sonnet sometimes scoped down.

**Impact:** Run 3: ~25 min in Reviewer (including terminal exploration, git commands, redundant script re-runs). Run 4: ~10 min in Reviewer (improved but still more than necessary for doc fixes).

##### 3.2.4 MEDIUM — Sequential fetching in generated code (Run 4)

**What happened:** `IssuesService.getIssueDetail()` called `issuesPort.getIssueDetail()` and `issuesPort.getLabelEvents()` sequentially despite having no data dependency. v2.3.3 code review rated this HIGH and refactored to `CompletableFuture` parallel execution.

**Why it happened:** The plan did not explicitly require parallel fetching. Neither the Coder nor the Reviewer flagged sequential execution of independent calls. The constitution says orchestration coordinates multi-source calls but doesn't prescribe parallel execution. `code-guidance.md` has no performance pattern guidance.

**Impact:** Doubled API latency for `GET /api/issues/{issueId}`. Fixed in refactoring.

##### 3.2.5 MEDIUM — Reviewer running scripts as primary activity (Runs 2, 3, 4)

**What happened:** Despite v2.3.0 instructions to "validate TL evidence instead of duplicating it" and "re-run only if TL evidence is suspect," the Reviewer continued to focus on terminal execution:
- Run 2 (Gemini): "No added value here almost"
- Run 3 (Codex): "bunch of terminal commands, not sure if there is any sense", ran git commands
- Run 4 (Sonnet): "Reviewer again is re-running the same scripts"

User: "Maybe our quality gate is a bit disproportional — only one final set of checks is needed, from Reviewer maybe we expect more code and best practices review and checking flaws and gaps. Otherwise he's expensive terminal runner without added value."

**Why it happened:** The Reviewer prompt lists evidence validation first, but then lists TL evidence artifact paths pointing to report files. The models interpret "validate evidence" as "re-execute commands and compare" rather than "read the evidence file and assess plausibility." The code quality checklist exists but is secondary to the verification checklist in prompt ordering.

**Impact:** Reviewer consumed 10-25 min per run with minimal code-quality value. The v2.3.3 code review (done by human + Opus) found 7 issues that the in-flow Reviewer missed entirely.

#### 3.3 Bottleneck Analysis

| Bottleneck | Evidence from run | Impact | Likely root cause |
|------------|-------------------|--------|-------------------|
| Coder handoff completeness | Rejected in runs 2, 3, 4. Run 3: 4 rejections, ~40 min wasted. Run 4: 1 rejection, ~15 min | 30-50% of total run time | Administrative tasks (report, context-map, verification log) compete with coding for model attention |
| Reviewer Phase 2 scope control | Run 3: full re-run on doc fix, ~25 min. Run 4: partial re-run, ~10 min | 10-20% of total run time | No delta-aware re-review mechanism in prompt |
| Architecture violation recurrence | Runs 3+4: same mapping-layer violation. Required post-run refactoring | Full code review + refactoring cycle after each run | Models' learned patterns override explicit constraints; Reviewer doesn't catch structural issues |
| Architect scanning time | Run 3: 20 min for plan (scanned dozens of files). Run 4: 6 min (context-map helped) | 5-15 min per run | Improving via context-map, but still room for improvement |
| TL verification overhead | Run 4: "27% context used — these checks are the most context-hungry for TL" | 8-12% context per run | `final-check.sh` + `karate-test.sh` produce large terminal output that fills TL context |

#### 3.4 Agent-by-Agent Assessment

| Agent | What went well | What went wrong | Recommendation |
|-------|----------------|-----------------|----------------|
| Team Lead (GPT-5.4) | Fast stage transitions; correct gate enforcement; never accepted broken code; read-only spot checks worked; no git commands; karate-test.sh integration clean | Context-hungry during verification (27% at peak); runs `mvn test` despite having `final-check.sh` (run 2); still the main context consumer at TL level | Reduce terminal output capture; use scripts that summarize results instead of dumping raw Maven output |
| Product Manager (Haiku) | Story in <1 sec (run 4); never failed after run 2; cheapest model; constitution awareness fixed post-run-3 | None significant | Keep as-is. This is the most efficient agent in the flow |
| Architect (Sonnet 4.6) | Plan in 6 min (run 4), improved from 20 min (run 3); file scanning reduced; context-map usage confirmed; Karate feature file writing works; 3 reasonable slices in run 4 | Run 3: 1 monolithic slice caused 4+ coder retries; plan is ~260 lines (vs 200 line cap); `Change.field()` as magic string not caught; no parallel-fetch requirement in plan | Consider explicit architecture patterns library; enforce plan line cap in Phase 1 |
| Coder (Codex/Sonnet) | Green code on first attempt (runs 1, 4b1); test quality acceptable; no mega-commands (improvement from v2.2.0); `verify-quick.sh` usage clean | Handoff artifact failure in every run (2, 3, 4); context-map updates forgotten; implementation report incomplete; run 3: 4 rejections from 1 monolithic slice | Split administrative handoff from coding; automate artifact generation |
| Reviewer (Codex→Sonnet) | Phase 1 consistently good; run 4 Phase 2 scoped down on re-review (Sonnet improvement); context-map mismatch caught (legitimate value) | Phase 2: still primarily a terminal runner; missed all v2.3.3 CRITICAL findings; Codex ran git commands (run 3); re-ran everything on doc fixes; "expensive terminal runner without added value" | Restructure Phase 2 prompt: code quality first, evidence validation second; add explicit architectural review checklist matching constitution principles; make re-review scope explicit |

└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

##### Actual Flow Deviations

- **Planning deviations:** None. PM, Architect, Phase 1 all passed first attempt. Best planning performance across all v2.3.0 runs.
- **Coder loops:** B1 passed first time (first clean intermediate handoff in v2.3.0 history). B2 passed first time. No code failures. One Phase 2 rejection for context-map and report incompleteness — administrative, not code.
- **Reviewer reruns:** 1 Phase 2 rejection (context-map + report artifact contradiction). Coder fix routed through TL. Second Phase 2 pass was scoped — Sonnet 4.6 only revalidated the specific findings, not the full checklist. This is an improvement over run 3.
- **Biggest single cost or quality impact:** The Reviewer Phase 2 round-trip (17:31→17:41) added 10 min for a documentation fix. Not catastrophic, but symptomatic of the "no delta review" design gap. The more damaging issue is invisible: Reviewer Phase 2 **missed** the 2 CRITICAL + 1 HIGH architecture findings that v2.3.3 code review later discovered.

### 4. Cost And Context Efficiency

#### 4.1 Cost Snapshot

| Cost dimension | Result | Notes |
|----------------|--------|-------|
| Total context used | 38% | Improved from run 3 (35% in 2h) but at faster pace — 38% in 1h20 |
| Premium requests used | Not precisely tracked | User: "these checks are the most context-hungry for TL" |
| Most expensive phase | Implementation (coder + TL verify) | 16:39→17:21 = 42 min, 6→27% context (21% of budget) |
| Most expensive agent | Coder (aggregate) | Two 17-20 min batches + 1 doc fix = ~40 min |
| Number of costly reruns / loops | 1 (Reviewer Phase 2 reject→fix→recheck) | 10 min, ~5% context |
| Cost efficiency verdict | **IMPROVED but not target** | 1h20 vs 2h (run 3) is real progress. Target is 30-40 min |

#### 4.2 Main Cost Drivers

1. **Coder batch duration (40 min, ~15% context):** Each batch takes 17-20 min. The code itself is produced in ~10 min; the remaining 7-10 min is verification execution, artifact updates, and handoff formatting. This is a 40-50% overhead.

2. **TL verification (10 min, ~10% context):** `final-check.sh` + `karate-test.sh` produce large terminal output. TL captures this output into its context window. This is the "most context-hungry" phase at TL level.

3. **Reviewer Phase 2 round-trip (10 min, ~5% context):** One rejection + fix + scoped recheck. This is the recurring pattern from runs 3 and 4.

4. **Architect scanning (6 min, ~3% context):** Improved from 20 min (run 3) but still higher than necessary. Context-map rewrite helped substantially.

5. **Plan artifact size (260 lines):** Exceeds the 200-line cap. Phase 1 Reviewer did not flag this. Larger plan = more context consumed by Coder when reading it.

#### 4.3 Cost Reduction Actions

| Action | Expected savings | Trade-off / risk |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| Automate handoff artifact generation (post-compile script that generates report skeleton + context-map diff) | 5-10 min per run; eliminate Reviewer Phase 2 rejection for admin issues | Script maintenance; need to keep template in sync |
| Add `--quiet` or summary-only mode to verification scripts so TL captures less terminal output | 3-5% context reduction at TL level | Less detail for debugging if failure occurs |
| Enforce plan line cap in Phase 1 Reviewer (reject plans >200 lines excl. payloads) | Smaller context footprint for Coder; ~2% context savings | Risk of over-compressed plans lacking critical detail |
| Pre-build Reviewer Phase 2 delta brief: when re-invoking after fix, TL specifies "recheck items X, Y only" | 5-10 min per re-review cycle | Requires TL prompt change; TL must correctly scope the delta |

### 5. Improvement Plan

#### 5.1 Priority Actions

| Priority | Action | Why it matters | Expected impact | Owner |
|----------|--------|----------------|-----------------|-------|
| P1 | Add architectural review checklist to Reviewer Phase 2 — explicit checks for constitution principles 2, 6, 8 (mapping boundaries, immutability, layer isolation) with code evidence requirements | Reviewer missed 2 CRITICAL + 1 HIGH architecture findings in run 4 that required post-run refactoring. This is the highest-cost failure mode | Catch architecture violations before sign-off; eliminate post-run refactoring cycles | `reviewer.agent.md` |
| P1 | Create handoff automation script: `scripts/coder-handoff-check.sh` that verifies context-map freshness, implementation report existence, and verification log existence before Coder returns to TL | Coder handoff artifact incompleteness caused rejections in runs 2, 3, 4. This is a mechanical check, not a judgment call — automate it | Eliminate 80%+ of Reviewer Phase 2 admin rejections; save 5-15 min per run | `scripts/`, `java-coder.agent.md` |
| P1 | Add delta-aware re-review mechanism: when TL re-invokes Reviewer after a scoped fix, include explicit scope in the prompt ("recheck only: context-map freshness, report consistency") | Reviewer Phase 2 full re-run on doc fixes wasted 10-25 min in runs 3-4 | 50% time reduction on Reviewer Phase 2 re-invocations | `team-lead.agent.md`, `reviewer.agent.md` |
| P1 | Add performance/composition patterns to `code-guidance.md`: "Independent port calls in orchestration SHOULD be parallelized via CompletableFuture" | Sequential fetching in run 4 doubled API latency. Required refactoring. Coder and Architect need explicit rules | Prevent sequential-fetch anti-pattern in future features | `documentation/code-guidance.md` |
| P2 | Add interface contract completeness rule to `code-guidance.md`: "Sealed interfaces and interface hierarchies MUST declare all common fields in the parent interface — implementations MUST NOT add publicly accessible fields not declared in the contract" | Run 4: `Change` had only `field()`, `LabelChange` added `id()` and `name()` without contract declaration. DTO diverged from model | Prevent thin interfaces and DTO-first development | `documentation/code-guidance.md` |
| P2 | Reduce TL verification output: modify scripts to output summary lines only (test count, pass/fail, report paths) and write verbose output to files | TL verification is the most context-hungry phase (user observation); raw Maven output fills context window | 3-5% context reduction per run; longer context runway for complex features | `scripts/final-check.sh`, `scripts/karate-test.sh` |
| P2 | Enforce plan line cap in Phase 1: Reviewer must flag plans exceeding 200 lines (excl. payloads) as `FAIL` | Run 4 plan was ~260 lines; Phase 1 did not flag it. Larger plans consume more Coder context | Smaller coder context footprint; more predictable batch timing | `reviewer.agent.md` |
| P3 | Consider GPT-5.4 for Architect | User: "Consider switch architect to GPT-5.4 — it can be faster and it's good when it works in boundaries." Sonnet 4.6 works well but scanning takes 6 min | Potentially faster plan generation | Model experiment |
| P3 | Add explicit DTO mirror rule: "DTO sealed interfaces MUST mirror the corresponding domain interface 1:1 — same method signatures, same hierarchy shape" | Run 4: `ChangeSetDto` was empty marker while `ChangeSet` had 4 methods | Prevent DTO/domain drift | `documentation/code-guidance.md` |

#### 5.2 Recommended Prompt / Workflow Changes

**Reviewer Phase 2 restructure (`reviewer.agent.md`):**
- Move Code Quality Review (naming, model count, duplication, over-engineering) to the **first** section, before evidence validation
- Add Constitution Compliance Review as a mandatory subsection: for each constitution principle relevant to the feature (especially 2, 6, 8), require explicit code evidence
- Add: "When re-invoked after a scoped fix, check only the items specified in the TL re-invocation prompt. Do not re-run the full Phase 2 checklist."
- Add: "If a plan defined interface hierarchies, verify that parent interfaces declare all common fields and implementations do not diverge"
- Mark terminal re-execution as the lowest-priority activity, not the starting point

**Team Lead delta brief (`team-lead.agent.md`):**
- When re-invoking Reviewer after a Coder fix, TL must include in the prompt: "Recheck only: [specific failed items]. All other Phase 2 items were PASS in the previous review."
- Add TL instruction: "Before Reviewer Phase 2, verify that `scripts/coder-handoff-check.sh` passes. Do not send to Reviewer if admin artifacts are missing."

**Coder handoff simplification (`java-coder.agent.md`):**
- Add: "Before returning handoff, run `scripts/coder-handoff-check.sh`. If it fails, fix the listed items before returning."
- Consider removing context-map update from Coder responsibility entirely and making it a TL post-batch task, since Coder consistently fails to update it properly

**Architect plan constraints (`java-architect.agent.md`):**
- Add: "When defining interface hierarchies (sealed interfaces, marker interfaces), explicitly state which methods belong to the parent contract vs. implementations. Do not leave parent interfaces with fewer methods than implementations — this creates unusable abstractions."
- Add: "When the orchestration service coordinates multiple independent port calls, explicitly state whether they should execute in parallel or sequentially, with reasoning."

#### 5.3 Recommended Tooling / Script Changes

1. **`scripts/coder-handoff-check.sh` (NEW):** Verify: implementation report exists and is valid JSON, verification log exists and has entries for all plan slices, context-map was modified more recently than production files, required fields in report are non-empty. Exit 0/1 with clear failure messages.

2. **`scripts/final-check.sh` summary mode:** Add `--summary` flag that outputs only: test count, pass/fail, quality gate status, and report paths. Write full output to `flow-orchestrator/target/final-check.log`. TL uses `--summary` by default.

3. **`scripts/karate-test.sh` summary mode:** Same pattern — summary to stdout, full output to `flow-orchestrator/target/karate-reports/karate-test.log`.

4. **Architecture pattern snippets:** Create `documentation/architecture-patterns.md` with canonical examples for: parallel port calls in service, sealed interface hierarchies with full contracts, DTO mirroring rules. Architect and Coder reference this instead of inventing patterns.

| Total run time | 1h 20min | 2h | 45 min |
| Context used | 38% | 35% | 30% |
| Planning phase time | 14 min | 25 min | 12 min |
| Coder batch time (avg) | 18 min | 50+ min (1 slice, 4 retries) | 12 min |
| Coder rejections | 0 code / 1 admin | 4+ (code + admin) | 0 |
| Reviewer Phase 2 reruns | 1 | 3 | 0 |
| Post-run refactoring findings | 2 CRITICAL + 1 HIGH + 2 MEDIUM | 1 CRITICAL (mapping layer) | 0 CRITICAL, ≤1 MEDIUM |
| Manual fixes after run | Full refactoring cycle (v2.3.3) | Mapping layer migration | ≤ minor naming/doc fixes |

### 6. Code Change Analysis

#### 6.1 Architecture and Boundaries

**As generated by run 4 (before v2.3.3 refactoring):**

- **Package structure:** Correct. All new files placed in proper packages per `code-guidance.md`. `orchestration/issues/model/` for domain contracts, `integration/gitlab/issues/dto/` for GitLab responses, `integration/gitlab/issues/mapper/` for GitLab-to-domain mapping.
- **Separation of concerns:** Mostly correct. `EnrichedIssueDetail` composition happens in `IssuesService` (orchestration) — this is the key fix from run 3. Integration adapter returns single-source data only.
- **Constitution violations found in v2.3.3 review:**
  - `Change` interface too thin (single `field()` method) — implementations carried contract-level data not declared in the parent (Principle 8: explicit mapping and model completeness)
  - `ChangeSetDto` empty marker — violated DTO-mirrors-domain expectation
  - Sequential port calls — violated composition efficiency (not explicitly in constitution but implied by multi-source coordination guidance)
- **Error handling:** Correct. `IntegrationException` for adapter failures, centralized `GlobalExceptionHandler` mapping. No raw exceptions leak.
- **Configuration:** Properly externalized. No new config concerns introduced.

**Post v2.3.3 refactoring:** All findings addressed. `Change` declares `field()`, `id()`, `name()` with `ChangeField` enum. `ChangeSetDto` is a sealed interface with full contract. Parallel fetching via `CompletableFuture`. Code is now architecturally clean.

#### 6.2 Maintainability and Readability

**Run 4 generated code quality (user observations):**
- "Incorrect modeling — orchestrator change model doesn't have all fields, and they added only on DTO which is extremely bad pattern"
- "Code looks completely auto-generated (much more than from previous attempt)"
- User challenged: defensive copy `labels = labels == null ? List.of() : List.copyOf(labels)` pattern — "hate this pattern" but it is actually consistent with `code-guidance.md` Defensive Copy Gate

**Naming:** Generally acceptable post-refactoring. `ChangeSet`, `Change`, `LabelChange`, `LabelChangeSet`, `ChangedBy` are all clear and business-oriented. `ChangeField` enum is a good pattern.

**Model count:** Appropriate. The sealed interface hierarchy (`Change` → `LabelChange`, `ChangeSet` → `LabelChangeSet`) is correctly extensible. No unnecessary wrappers.

**Auto-generated feel:** The defensive copy pattern is repetitive but necessary per the project's own rules. The real "auto-generated" concern is about incomplete interface contracts — models default to the minimum viable interface rather than the complete contract.

#### 6.3 Testing and Verification Quality

**Strong:**
- 3-tier test placement correct (unit / integration / component)
- Component tests with WireMock stub support classes are clean and maintainable
- Adapter tests use `MockRestServiceServer` appropriately
- Karate smoke tests cover all endpoints including lifecycle scenarios
- 110 surefire + 35 failsafe tests all green
- JaCoCo coverage gate green (85%+ required)

**Gaps found in v2.3.3 review:**
- Missing edge-case tests for label event mapping (required `user`, `action`, `created_at` field tests)
- Missing multi-event ordering test
- Missing multi-assignee and nullable-assignee tests in issue-detail mapper
- Component test didn't verify label-events endpoint is called during parallel fetch when issue detail is 404

**Assessment:** Test infrastructure is solid. Edge-case coverage is below what a senior human developer would produce. The `test-instructions.instructions.md` rule "each plan slice must include at least one edge-case or boundary-condition test" is followed mechanically but not deeply.

#### 6.4 Risks and Follow-Up Refactors

| Risk | Why it matters | Recommended follow-up |
|------|----------------|-----------------------|
| Architecture violations may recur in next feature | Same mapping-layer violation happened in runs 3 and 4 despite explicit fixes. Models' learned patterns override prompt constraints | Add architecture patterns doc with canonical examples; add constitution compliance check to Reviewer Phase 2 |
| Interface contract completeness not enforced | `Change` was underspecified; DTO diverged from domain. Code-guidance has no explicit rule about parent interface completeness | Add interface contract rule to `code-guidance.md` |
| Sequential port calls as default | Parallel execution not mentioned in plan; Coder defaulted to sequential. Will recur for every multi-source composition | Add parallel composition guidance to `code-guidance.md`; Architect must specify execution strategy in plan |
| Edge-case test depth | Automated tests pass but miss important edge cases consistently | Consider adding "edge-case verification" as an explicit Reviewer Phase 2 item with examples from previous code reviews |
| Run time still 2x target | 1h20 vs 30-40 min target. If next feature is larger, risk of 2h+ run | Address P1 items (handoff automation, delta-aware review, script summary modes) before next run |

### 7. Final Verdict

**v2.3.0 is the best version to date** and should be kept as the new baseline. The planning phase is mature, the quality tooling is reliable, and the workflow design is sound. The gap is in execution, not design.

**What should definitely remain:**
- PM (Haiku) — fastest, cheapest, most reliable
- Planning pipeline (PM → Architect → Phase 1 Reviewer) — consistently 10-14 min
- Karate test infrastructure with `karate-test.sh` auto-start/reuse
- Spotless + `final-check.sh` + `quality-check.sh` toolchain
- Context-map as navigation index for agents
- TL gate enforcement (no false-positive passes in v2.3.0)
- Constitution awareness in PM and Phase 1 Reviewer

**What should be changed before the next run (v2.4.0):**

1. **Reviewer Phase 2 must become an architecture reviewer, not a terminal runner.** This is the single highest-leverage change. The Reviewer missed every issue that the v2.3.3 code review found. Restructure Phase 2: code quality and constitution compliance first, evidence validation second, terminal execution last.

2. **Automate handoff artifact checks.** The Coder → TL → Reviewer rejection loop for missing report/context-map is a solved problem if automated. Create `scripts/coder-handoff-check.sh`.

3. **Add delta-aware re-review.** When Reviewer Phase 2 is re-invoked for a scoped fix, TL must specify the scope. Reviewer must honor it.

4. **Expand `code-guidance.md`** with: interface contract completeness, parallel composition pattern, DTO mirror rules. These are the specific gaps that caused v2.3.3 refactoring.

5. **Reduce TL context consumption** via summary-mode scripts.

**Trend assessment:** The system is **trending toward better quality and efficiency**, but at a rate that will require 2-3 more version iterations to reach the 30-40 min target. The planning phase improvement trajectory is steep (38 min → 14 min across 4 runs). The implementation phase improvement trajectory is slower (1h40 → 40 min for v2.2.0→v2.3.0 run 4, but still 2x target). The biggest risk is not time — it is that the Reviewer fails to catch architecture issues that require post-run refactoring, making the true cost of a run = run time + refactoring time.

## Research Summary

- Reused the shared run-log template as the baseline for the new version log.
- Seeded the first `Version Updates` entry with the new template adoption.
- Framed the change explicitly around improved observability across runs.
- Full analysis performed against all 4 run observations, v2.2.0 prior analysis, v2.3.3 code review findings, all agent files, all instruction files, code-guidance, constitution, context-map, implementation artifacts, and generated source code.
- Architecture violations confirmed by reading actual source: `Change.java` (post-refactoring has `field()`, `id()`, `name()`; pre-refactoring had only `field()`), `IssuesService.java` (post-refactoring uses `CompletableFuture`; pre-refactoring was sequential), `IssueDetailDto.java` (post-refactoring has sealed interfaces with full contracts).
- v2.3.3 refactoring verified: all 5 implemented findings confirmed in code, 117 tests green, all quality gates pass.
